24: hos tas hamartias hemon autos anenegken en to somati autou epi to xylon (hos tas hamartias hēmōn autos anēnenken en tō sōmati autou epi to xylon): 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree.' The language is saturated with Isa 53: anaphero is the LXX word for the priestly act of lifting/bearing an offering to the altar; epi to xylon (on the tree) echoes Deut 21:23 (a hanged man is cursed) and Gal 3:13 (Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us). The phrase 'in his body' is a deliberate anti-Docetic marker: the sin-bearing was physical, not merely spiritual — the crucifixion was real.
1: apothemenoi — strip off/put away (aorist middle participle: a decisive action, like removing a garment — the same clothing metaphor as Eph 4:22, Col 3:8). The five vices: kakian (malice), dolon (deceit), hypokrisias (hypocrisies), phthonous (envies), katalalias (slanders). The list moves from internal attitude (malice, deceit) to performed disguise (hypocrisies) to competitive vice (envies) to destructive speech (slanders) — a social anatomy of community breakdown.
2: artigennēta brephē — newborn infants (continuing the birth-imagery of 1:23); logikon adolon gala — pure/unadulterated rational/word-milk. Logikon is deliberately ambiguous: (1) rational milk — milk appropriate to reasoning beings; (2) milk of the Word (logos) — given Peter's emphasis on the living logos in 1:23-25, both senses likely operate. Adolos = unadulterated, without guile — the same virtue asked of the community (guile listed as a vice in v.1); the milk itself models the character sought.
3: ei egeusasthe — if you have tasted (aorist — a completed past experience: having once tasted); hoti chrēstos ho Kyrios — that the Lord is good/kind. In Greek, chrēstos (good, kind) is a near-homophone of Christos (Christ); early Christian readers likely heard the wordplay deliberately: "if you have tasted that Christ is Christ." The verse cites Ps 34:8 LXX and connects tasting the Lord to coming to the living Stone of v.4.
4: pros hon proserchomenoi — coming to whom (present participle — the coming is ongoing); lithos zōn — living stone (an oxymoron in natural terms: stones are dead material; a living stone combines the solidity of stone with the vitality of life). apo anthrōpōn men apodedokimasmenon — having been rejected by humans after testing (apodokimazō = to reject after examination — the same word used in Ps 118:22 LXX; the builders who tested and discarded the stone); para Theō de eklekton entimon — but before God chosen and precious.
5: kai autoi hōs lithoi zōntes oikodomeisthe — you also as living stones are being built (present passive — the building is ongoing; the believers share the attribute of the cornerstone: living stones forming a spiritual house). oikos pneumatikos — a spiritual house (not made of physical material; the temple-imagery — God dwelling among his people — is realized in the community). hierateuma hagion — holy priesthood: the community does collectively what the Levitical priesthood did institutionally; pneumatikas thysias — spiritual sacrifices (not animal blood but the offering of lives and praise).
6: The Isaiah 28:16 LXX citation introduces the stone catena. akrogōniaion — cornerstone (literally head-corner-stone; the foundational stone that determines the alignment of all others). ho pisteuōn ep'autō ou mē kataischynthē — the one believing on him will by no means be put to shame (the ou mē double negative is emphatic — an absolute denial of shame for the believer). The honor-shame world of Peter's readers makes this promise significant: the rejected stone (= social shame) is actually the chosen cornerstone (= divine honor).
7: hymin oun hē timē — to you therefore the honor/preciousness (timē = honor and price; the same word as entimon in v.4 — the stone is precious to God; this preciousness/honor comes to believers through their relationship to the stone). The Ps 118:22 LXX citation: ho lithos hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomoūntes, houtos egenēthē eis kephalēn gōnias — the stone which the builders rejected became the head of the corner. The reversal is total: rejection → supremacy.
8: The Isa 8:14 citation: lithos proskommatos kai petra skandalou — stone of stumbling and rock of offense. Proskomma (stumbling block — what the foot strikes against) and skandalon (trap-stick — the triggering stick of an animal trap). The same stone that is the corner for believers is the stumbling-stone for unbelievers; the difference is not in the stone but in the posture of approach. hoi apeithountes — those disobeying the word: disobedience to the word, not merely disbelief, is what makes Christ a stumbling stone.
9: Four OT titles applied to the new community: genos eklekton (chosen race — Isa 43:20), basileion hierateuma (royal priesthood — Exod 19:6 LXX, where basileion is ambiguous: palace/kingdom of priests or royal/kingly priesthood), ethnos hagion (holy nation — Exod 19:6), laos eis peripoiēsin (people for possession/acquisition — peripoiēsis = personal possession, the LXX equivalent of Hebrew segullah, Exod 19:5). All four titles were Israel's covenant identity; all four are now applied to the multi-ethnic community of believers. Aretās exangeilatē — you might declare the excellences/virtues: aretai is the Greek word for human excellence; applied to God's deeds, it reframes divine acts as demonstrations of divine excellence.
10: hoi pote ou laos, nyn de laos Theou — once not a people, now the people of God (Hosea 1:9 + 2:23 — the not-my-people → my-people reversal). The Hosea background is Israel's rejection and restoration through covenant faithfulness; Peter applies the restoration language to communities that were never formally within the Mosaic covenant at all. ouk ēleēmenoi, nyn de eleēthentes — having not received mercy, but now having received mercy (eleeō twice — the mercy-vocabulary of the covenant).
11: paroikous kai parepidēmous — resident aliens and sojourners (two civic-status terms of increasing marginalization). A paroikos had some recognized status as a resident alien; a parepidēmos was even more transient — a visitor with no lasting legal tie. Peter uses both terms to describe the community's actual social condition and reframe it theologically: their marginalized status corresponds to their true identity as those whose home is elsewhere. sarkinōn epithymiōn... strateuesthai kata tēs psychēs — fleshly desires waging war against the soul — military language for the internal conflict.
12: anastrophēn kalēn echontes en tois ethnesin — having good conduct among the nations/Gentiles; anastrophē (way of life, conduct — the key word of the letter) in public view among outsiders. katēgorousin — they slander (present tense — ongoing); epopteusantes — having observed (epoptēs was the technical term for one who had been initiated to view the mysteries; Peter may be playing on the mystery-religion vocabulary: the pagan observers become involuntary witnesses to the Christian way of life).
13: hypotagēte pasē anthrōpinē ktisei — submit to every human institution (ktisis = creature/creation; the phrase means all human authority structures as institutions of human society). dia ton Kyrion — for the Lord's sake (the reason for submission is not political quietism but the Lord's own authority over the public order). basilei hōs hyperechonti — to the king as supreme (hyperechō = to be above/supreme — the political language of hierarchy).
14: Governors (hēgemosin) sent by the king to perform two functions: eis ekdikēsin kakōn (for punishment of evildoers) and epainon agathopoiōn (for praise of those doing good). The theory of government presupposed is the Roman administrative ideal that provincial governors punish vice and reward virtue — the Christian community's good behavior should fit the "reward" category, not the "punishment" category.
15: hoti houtōs estin to thelēma tou Theou — for this is the will of God (the will of God grounds the ethic of good conduct — not mere pragmatism but divine purpose). agathopoiountas phimoun tēn tōn aphronōn anthrōpōn agnōsian — by doing good to muzzle the ignorance of foolish people (phimoō = to muzzle; used for silencing animals; the good deeds silence the slanders as effectively as a muzzle).
16: hōs eleutheroi — as free people (the freedom is real, not merely metaphorical); kai mē echontes tēn eleutherian eis epikalymma tēs kakias — and not using freedom as a covering for evil (epikalymma = covering/veil — the freedom that becomes a disguise for self-indulgence betrays its own nature). hōs Theou douloi — as God's slaves/servants — the paradox: freedom is expressed through willing service to God, not through autonomous self-determination.
17: Four rapid imperatives without connective particles (asyndeton): timēsate pantas (honor all people), tēn adelphotēta agapate (love the brotherhood), ton Theon phobeisthe (fear/revere God), ton basilea timathe (honor the king). The structure is chiastic in scope: all → brotherhood → God → king, with the two honor-commands framing two inner commands. The double use of timaō for both "all people" and "the king" prevents the emperor from claiming unique honor; the king is honored as one instance of honoring all people, not as a unique category.
18: oiketai — household servants (oiketēs = a domestic/house servant, more specific than doulos — these are household staff, not agricultural or commercial slaves). en panti phobō — in all reverence (the phobos here is the fear of God from 1:17, not necessarily fear of the master — the next verse makes this explicit: dia syneīdēsin Theou). tois skoliois — to the crooked ones (skolios = bent, crooked — describing morally twisted masters).
19: touto gar charis — for this is grace/commendable (charis here functions as "what is creditable/acceptable to God" — not merely natural grace but the specifically theological quality of action aligned with God's own character). dia syneīdēsin Theou — through awareness/consciousness of God (syneidēsis = conscience, co-knowing; the awareness of God that shapes the response to unjust suffering).
20: poion gar kleos — for what kleos (fame, glory — the Greek honor-concept for achieving noteworthy reputation)? The question uses the vocabulary of Greco-Roman achievement culture: enduring beating for wrongdoing earns no honor-credit in any value system. agathopoiountas kai paschontas — doing good and suffering: this combination — good conduct still producing suffering — is the situation of Christ and of Peter's community. Touto charis para Theō — this is acceptable with God.
21: eis touto gar eklēthēte — for to this you were called (the calling is to the path of unjust suffering, not away from it). hypogrammon — a copybook (literally: writing under a model; the schoolchild's exercise of tracing over the teacher's letters to learn the correct form; the unique NT use of this word makes Christ's life the pattern that must be traced over). hina epakolouthēsēte tois ichnesin autou — that you might follow in his tracks (ichnē = footprints — the marks left by a person's feet).
22: Isaiah 53:9 LXX: hamartian ouk epoiēsen, oude heurethē dolos en tō stomati autou — no sin committed, no deceit found in his mouth. The two absences are absolute: no wrongdoing (acted) and no deceit (spoken) — the two modes of offense against others. The citation follows the Isaiah 53 Servant pattern that runs through vv.21-25 without formal introduction; Peter assumes his readers recognize the intertextual register.
23: loidoroumenos ouk anteloidorei — being insulted he did not counter-insult (antiloidoreō = to insult in return); paschōn ouk ēpeilei — suffering he did not threaten. Two present participles + two imperfect negatives: the ongoing suffering of Christ produced no reactive violence in return. paredidou de tō krinonti dikaiōs — but kept entrusting himself to the one judging justly (imperfect: a sustained act of trust rather than a single moment; paradidōmi = to hand over, entrust).
25: poimena kai episkopon tōn psychōn hymōn — Shepherd and Overseer/Guardian of your souls. The two titles are combined: the Shepherd of Ezek 34 (who seeks, feeds, and protects the flock) and the episkopos (one who watches over, supervises — the same word for the office of overseer in the churches). eplanuomenoi — straying/wandering (the Isa 53:6 sheep-going-astray image; the perfect passive: you were in the state of having strayed; now you have returned).
